author: NoddyTnT
comment_content: The course covers three main areas requiring solid ELEC background and SSH skills for Raspberry Pi work. Signal processing involves chirps, frequency domain, FFT, and TDOA concepts (ELEC1200 knowledge helpful but rusty for many). Wireless communication covers protocols like WiFi, ALOHA, and LoRa (ELEC3120 beneficial but not essential as professor explains thoroughly). Human activity recognition introduces machine learning with sensor data, taught by a different professor making it harder to follow.
comment_teaching: Labs 1-3 focus on signal processing using FFT codes, sin/cos functions, and Doppler effects to generate graphs and determine 2D object positioning - setup is lengthy but straightforward. Labs 4-6 explore LoRa features, Arduino coding for retransmission, and campus signal coverage testing across floors 3-5. Labs 7-8 provide PyTorch introduction and model improvement exercises for higher marks.
comment_grading: Assessment includes class participation (10%), midterm exam (30%), individual lab projects (30% total, 10% each), and group project (30%) covering proposal, execution, demonstration, and report. The open-notes midterm is quite difficult, covering lab content like Free Space Path Loss with incomplete derivation explanations, plus questions on chirps, ALOHA, and FSPL concepts.
comment_workload: Extremely high workload, especially overwhelming for students with limited programming background beyond basic Python. The LoRa and PyTorch sections are particularly brutal without prior experience, requiring significant time investment to understand packet structures, ACK protocols, and machine learning implementations. Projects start dangerously late in the semester with zero budget provided, forcing students to rush through complex implementations while scrambling for resources. The combination of individual lab assignments demanding full concept mastery and group projects requiring coordination creates a perfect storm of stress. Success heavily depends on forming strong study groups for homework discussions and securing reliable, knowledgeable groupmates - having a disappearing team member makes the already challenging assignments nearly impossible to complete solo. Students often find themselves pulling all-nighters trying to bridge knowledge gaps while simultaneously working on multiple demanding assignments.

author: sheepy
title: I have a COMP. I have an ELEC. uhh -- COMP4531
comment_content:
See the earlier review for the exact content.
Definitely need some background in ELEC before to deal with the Rasberry Pi and its compatible components. (and SSH as we need it for connection throughout the labs and projects)
Basically:
1. Signal
->some signal processing related thing like: chirps,frequency domain,FFT,TDOA
(I studied ELEC1200 long ago and I forgot a lot of the concepts here)
2. Wireless communication
->protocols and techniques in wireless network like : Wifi, ALOHA, LoRa
(would be better if you have studied e.g. ELEC3120 before but not a must, as the Prof will explain things like ALOHA in detail)
3. Human activity recognition
->introduction to machine learning and how it works with sensor data to give out predictions
(A different Prof taught this part so I can barely catch up with the progress)
and other technologies

Lab/Assignment:
1-3. Signal processing
->use simple codes with FFT,sin/cos, Doppler effects to print out s-t and v-t graphs to determine object movements
->the prior set up is quite long but easy
->write own code to determine 2D position of object
4-6. LoRa
->explain some special features on LoRA transmission process and packets
->write some Arduino codes for retransmission or lower SF for strengthening connections
->explore the LoRa signal coverage/strength within the campus (3-5/F)
7-8. Machine learning
->brief introduction to PyTorch
->explore and improve the given model to earn higher marks
comment_teaching:
The professor teach quite well and his explanation is simple. Without much prior knowledge, I can still catch up with the concepts and math derivations in class.

But the lab/tutorial session is a lot harder with pratical uses of signal processing, LoRa protocals, and machine learning. The TAs basically read through the slides so the details need to be self-taught.

The lab session is too short and do not have much time for further explain of concepts or discussion.
comment_grading:
Class involvement and discussion 10%
Mid-term exam 30%
Lab projects(individual)  30%    (10% per assignment)
Course project (group based, ~3 students per group)                                                          
Project proposal, execution, demonstration and report   30%

The midterm is quite hard as it involves many lab contents and concepts like Free space path loss that is introduced in labs and the derivations are not fully explained. It is open-notes and contains questions on chirps, ALOHA, FSPL, etc.

The grades turn out quite good despite the fact that my work is terrible.
comment_workload:
Very high workload as I only have python background from 1021 intro to turtles. :[

The LoRa and Pytorch part is quite hard for those who have little background. The project started very late so we have to rush for it with no budget provided :[  Gladly I got cool groupmates with prior knowledge in YOLO and I used some old library for the ReSpeaker on top on it to deal with the project.

Yet, the workload is quite high as the lab assignments requires you to fully understand the concepts of signal, LoRa (packets structure and ACK), and how to implement models based on the simple lab works. Try to get yourself friends to discuss the HW and project. I got a disappearing groupmate for the lab and it is quite hard to finish the assignment by yourself.

author: i_dun_know
title: More like an ELEC Course than CS course
comment_content:
For ELEC/CPEG students: This is a course to let you know how the concepts you learn from ELEC1200, ELEC2100 works in real life.

On canvas what he said:
List of Topics
1.Introduction of Internet-of-Things
2.Sensing and communication basics
    2.1 Introduction to sensing and sensors
    2.2 Wireless sensing and communication techniques
3.Wireless networking
4.Applications
    4.1 Localization & tracking
    4.2 Human activity recognition
    4.3 RFID and others
5.Security and privacy

WARNING:
It is strongly recommend to take the following courses before enroll.
ELEC1200 A System View of Communications: From Signals to Packets (Related to Topic 2,3)
ELEC2100 Signals and Systems (Related to Topic 2,3)
ELEC3100 Signal Processing and Communications (Related to Topic 2,3)
ELEC3120/COMP 4621 Computer Communication Network (Related to Topic 1, 3)

In this year, the midterm exam covers from Topic 1 to Topic 3 and it is an in class open book exam with 3 questions. The time limit of the exam is 60 minutes. I would say the exam is kinda easy, the answers can easily find in the notes.
comment_teaching:
Having his lesson just like opening a mystery box, you will never know what he going to teach in the coming lesson as he will only upload the slides once he finished the whole chapter. However, the content and his teaching is wonderful.

His TAs are so nice and quick respond.
comment_grading:
Grading Scheme

10%    Class involvement and discussion*   (Did not tell)                                                   
30%    Mid-term exam. (mean 71.35/110, me 65/110)                                                                                  
30%    Lab projects * 3 (individual)
           Asm 1: (mean: 8/10, me 10/10)
           Asm 2: (mean: 8.21/10, me 8/10)
           Asm 3: (mean: 8.63/10, me 10/10)
30%    Course project (group based, At most 4 students per group)                                                          
            Report: 10%
            Q&A: 10%
            Demo & Present: 10%
            Total mean:  25.85/30
            Me: 30/30

Final Grade: A

*For the class involvement, he will make down the name(s) of the student(s) who answer his question or ask question every lesson.
comment_workload:
There are 3 assignment after every 3 labs. In the lab sections, the TA will introduce the concepts related to assignments and you may try to do it in there.

The main workload of this course is the project. The details of the project were given in the end of week 9. We have to submit the proposal within 1 week, and demo the project within 4 weeks. Also, the report deadline is before the demo and presentation.
